topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door made me jump, my hand instinctively flying to cover my mouth. A delivery person, looking harried, was holding a box addressed to 'Apartment 3B'. My stomach clenched. I knew instantly who it was for. It was the previous tenant, Mrs. Henderson, and the package was clearly meant for her. My face felt hot, a flush creeping up my neck. I fumbled with the lock, my movements clumsy and overly exaggerated as I tried to manage the situation. When the delivery person politely explained the mix-up, I could only manage a strained, high-pitched apology, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. I quickly ushered them away, retreating into my apartment as if trying to disappear into the wallpaper.

[story 2]

I found the package sitting on my doorstep, a bulky cardboard box with a faded label. My breath hitched. It was clearly addressed to the unit above mine—the one that had just been vacated. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over me, making my palms sweat. I stood there for a full minute, my gaze darting everywhere but at the box, trying to construct a plausible explanation for why it had ended up in my hands. When the mail carrier called out, my voice came out thin and reedy. "Oh, uh, I think this might be for someone else," I mumbled, trying to sound casual while my entire body felt rigid with self-consciousness. I snatched the package up, my fingers trembling slightly, and hurried to take it down the hall, desperate to get it out of sight.

[story 3]

The doorbell rang, and I peered through the peephole. It was a delivery driver, holding a parcel clearly marked for the vacant unit next door. A sudden, heavy weight settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I wanted the ground to open up and swallow my shoes. I opened the door slowly, my posture stiff, trying to appear nonchalant while my internal alarm bells were ringing loudly. "Excuse me," I managed, my voice sounding strangely strained, "this isn't for me." I watched the driver's expression shift from helpful to slightly bewildered, and I felt a burning sensation behind my eyes. I quickly ushered him back onto the stoop, pulling the door shut with a decisive click, wishing desperately for the pavement to simply vanish.
